proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that restoration strategies prioritizing native, climate-adapted vegetation and soil microbiome enhancement will yield a net positive economic return over a 30-year horizon, when accounting for the monetary value of carbon sequestration, reduced future fire hazard, and biodiversity co-benefits.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 datasets on vegetation recovery, soil carbon, and fire vulnerability with new field surveys across a Sierra Nevada wildfire chronosequence. Using a process-based ecosystem model (e.g., ED2) calibrated with pre- and post-fire microbial gene expression and carbon degradation data, we will simulate restoration scenarios. A Monte Carlo-based economic model will quantify costs (planting, management) and benefits, valuing carbon using social cost of carbon estimates and fire risk reduction via avoided property damage and suppression costs from historical fire databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated biophysical-economic framework to quantify the return on investment for forest restoration in fire-prone systems. It will deliver a scalable decision-support tool for land managers and policymakers to prioritize restoration actions that maximize climate mitigation and adaptation benefits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly coupling microbial functional data and carbon degradation dynamics with economic valuation of fire risk reduction, moving beyond simple carbon-centric analyses to a holistic assessment of ecosystem resilience.

proposals.title: System Dynamics Modeling of Fire-Carbon-Climate Feedbacks to Evaluate Forest Management and Climate Restoration Policies in California
proposals.motivation: California's forests are critical carbon sinks increasingly threatened by climate-driven increases in wildfire frequency and severity, as documented in pre-2026 research. Current policy evaluations often fail to capture the complex, long-term feedbacks between fire regimes, post-fire ecosystem recovery, soil carbon dynamics, and climate, leading to potentially suboptimal or maladaptive management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system dynamics model integrating climate drivers, vegetation succession, fire behavior, and soil carbon pools will reveal non-linear thresholds and time-lagged effects, demonstrating that policies focused solely on immediate fire suppression or carbon sequestration are less effective than integrated strategies for long-term climate restoration.
proposals.method: We will develop a system dynamics model using the Stella Architect platform. Key stocks will include live biomass (modeled with species-specific traits from recent genomic and vegetation studies), soil organic carbon (partitioned into labile and recalcitrant pools informed by microbial degradation studies), and a fire vulnerability index. Climate forcing will use CMIP6 projections. Feedback loops will explicitly link fire frequency to fuel loads, deposition (e.g., post-fire nutrient fluxes), and vegetation community shifts. Policy levers (e.g., prescribed fire, thinning, assisted migration) will be modeled as exogenous controls, with outcomes evaluated over a 50-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated framework to quantify trade-offs and synergies between carbon storage and fire resilience under different policy scenarios. It will directly contribute to the field by translating mechanistic understanding of post-fire photosynthetic recovery and community vulnerability into a decision-support tool for evaluating the long-term efficacy of climate restoration policies.
proposals.novelty: While system dynamics is established in engineering, its application to integrate pre-2026 knowledge on microbial soil processes, genetic traits of vulnerability, and climate-driven fire risk for policy evaluation in forest ecosystems is novel. This moves beyond correlative or equilibrium models to capture the dynamic feedbacks central to the fire-carbon-climate nexus.

proposals.title: Regional-scale microbial and vegetation interventions for permafrost carbon stabilization: A testbed for scalable Arctic restoration
proposals.motivation: Arctic permafrost thaw, accelerated by climate change and increasing fire frequency, threatens to release vast soil carbon stocks, creating a dangerous positive feedback loop. Pre-2026 research highlights the vulnerability of these carbon pools but lacks scalable, ecologically grounded intervention strategies that can be regionally implemented and monitored before considering planetary-scale deployment. This work addresses a critical gap between mechanistic understanding of microbial degradation and vegetation recovery and actionable, large-scale restoration frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted regional interventions combining fire-adapted vegetation restoration with microbial community management can significantly enhance soil carbon stability in thawing Arctic landscapes, and the effectiveness of these interventions can be predicted and scaled using integrated climate-ecosystem models.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-2026 methods: 1) Using high-resolution climate and fire regime projections to identify vulnerable Arctic regions. 2) Applying gene sequencing and metagenomics to characterize microbial communities responsible for decomposition across a gradient of fire history and vegetation recovery states. 3) Employing a process-based ecosystem model (e.g., a modified ED2 or CLM) to simulate carbon fluxes, explicitly incorporating empirical data on microbial functional genes and post-fire vegetation trajectories. 4) Designing and modeling regional-scale intervention scenarios (e.g., seeding of deep-rooted, fire-resilient vegetation, microbial inoculants) to test their impact on long-term soil carbon storage.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a first-of-its-kind, mechanistic framework for designing and prioritizing regional Arctic restoration aimed at carbon stabilization. It will directly link microbial degradation pathways with vegetation-based solutions, moving beyond observation to actionable intervention strategies. The work will establish a critical testbed for evaluating the feasibility and side-effects of biogeochemical restoration before global deployment is considered.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied Arctic carbon vulnerability or microbial processes in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates microbial genomics, post-fire vegetation recovery, and ecosystem modeling to engineer and test scalable regional restoration protocols, shifting the paradigm from risk assessment to proactive ecological management.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Climate Targets to Define Fire-Resilient and Carbon-Stable States for California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Current climate projections largely forecast ecosystem vulnerability, offering limited guidance on actionable targets for ecosystem management. Given the increasing frequency and severity of wildfires in California, there is an urgent need to define specific, acceptable future climate states that maintain key ecosystem functions like carbon sequestration and community stability. This inverse approach shifts the paradigm from predicting impacts to defining the conditions necessary for resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given California ecosystem, there exists a quantifiable set of climate parameters (e.g., temperature, precipitation, vapor pressure deficit) that, if achieved, would maintain fire regimes within historical ranges of variation and support stable soil carbon pools. The required biogeochemical and vegetation interventions to reach this state can be back-calculated from this target.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse modeling framework using a Bayesian calibration of the Community Land Model (CLM) with coupled fire and biogeochemistry modules. First, we will define 'acceptable' states using pre-2026 data on historical fire return intervals, soil carbon stocks, and vegetation composition from Field's prior work. The inverse solver will use a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to identify the multivariate climate space meeting these ecosystem constraints. Finally, we will use the GENECOS vegetation model to simulate the microbial and degradation pathways necessary for transitioning contemporary, climate-vulnerable systems (e.g., low-elevation Sierra conifers) toward the target state.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, ecosystem-specific climate targets designed for fire and carbon resilience, moving beyond generic warming limits. It will provide a novel decision-support tool for land managers, linking macroclimate goals to required interventions in vegetation management, microbial community enhancement, and carbon deposition strategies.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of climate impacts on fire and carbon is common, inverting the problem to solve for a permissible climate state based on ecosystem thresholds is a fundamentally different and untapped approach. It integrates Field's expertise in fire-carbon-community dynamics with inverse climate modeling, a method not yet applied to defining actionable ecosystem climate targets.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Projections of Post-Fire Carbon Allocation and Resilience in California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Post-fire ecosystem recovery is a critical but uncertain component of the terrestrial carbon cycle, with allocation of carbon to roots, wood, and leaves determining resilience to subsequent stressors like drought. Current dynamic vegetation models often use fixed allocation parameters, failing to capture the observed plasticity and uncertainty in plant responses to interacting environmental drivers such as altered nutrient deposition, climate extremes, and fire frequency. Quantifying this uncertainty is essential for predicting carbon sequestration trajectories and vulnerability thresholds in fire-prone systems like California's.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating environmental drivers (soil nutrients, post-fire climate) into probability distributions for carbon allocation coefficients will reveal that the uncertainty in biomass recovery pathways increases non-linearly beyond specific stressor thresholds, and that this probabilistic approach will identify communities at highest risk of allocation failure (e.g., insufficient root investment).
proposals.method: We will fuse a dynamic carbon allocation model (e.g., based on Allometric Growth Theory) with a Monte Carlo ensemble framework. For key California ecosystems (e.g., Sierra Nevada conifers, chaparral), we will define prior probability distributions for allocation parameters, informed by pre-2026 trait databases and Field's prior work on post-fire photosynthesis. These priors will be conditioned on environmental drivers (downscaled CMIP5 climate forecasts, soil nutrient/deposition maps). Thousands of simulations will be run via Latin Hypercube Sampling to propagate input and parameter uncertainty, generating probabilistic clouds of biomass outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first uncertainty-quantified projections of post-fire carbon partitioning, directly linking environmental stress to the probability of specific recovery trajectories. It will provide a novel framework for assessing ecosystem resilience, identifying where and when model predictions are most unreliable, and informing trait-based vulnerability assessments under global change.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond deterministic sensitivity analysis by formally treating carbon allocation strategies as probabilistic, environmentally responsive traits, and in fusing ensemble methods with dynamic allocation models to define risk landscapes for ecosystem recovery.

proposals.title: Non-additive interactions of temperature, radiation, and precipitation drive post-fire phenological recovery: A pharmacodynamic modeling approach
proposals.motivation: Current models of plant phenology under climate change, especially in fire-prone ecosystems, often assume additive effects of climatic drivers, potentially misrepresenting ecosystem vulnerability. Post-fire recovery trajectories, a key determinant of carbon sink resilience, are influenced by concurrent climate stresses whose interactions are poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict phenological shifts and associated carbon cycling in systems like California's fire-impacted forests and shrublands.
proposals.hypothesis: The combined effects of temperature, solar radiation, and precipitation on post-disturbance phenological events (e.g., green-up, flowering) are non-additive, exhibiting significant synergistic or antagonistic interactions that can be quantified using pharmacodynamic interaction indices.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing pre-2026 remote sensing phenology data (e.g., MODIS, Landsat) and gridded climate data for California ecosystems with documented fire histories. For key vegetation types, we will fit empirical response surfaces using generalized additive models (GAMs) to describe phenological timing as a function of temperature, radiation, and precipitation. We will then calculate interaction indices, specifically the Loewe Additivity model and the Combination Index, to statistically characterize and map regions of significant synergy or antagonism among drivers, validating against field-based phenological observations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit maps of climate driver interactions on phenology in fire-affected ecosystems, directly informing more mechanistic dynamic vegetation models (e.g., LPJ, ED2). It will provide a novel diagnostic framework to assess whether ecosystems are experiencing compounded climate stresses, thereby refining vulnerability assessments for carbon stocks and community composition.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of pharmacodynamic interaction models, a well-established framework in toxicology, to the analysis of concurrent climate drivers on phenology represents a significant conceptual and methodological crossover. This moves beyond correlation-based approaches to a formal test of interaction, challenging the pervasive assumption of additivity in bioclimatic studies.

proposals.title: Interactive Climate Drivers of Post-Fire Phenological Recovery: A Multi-Scale Modeling Approach for California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Current models of post-fire ecosystem recovery, including photosynthetic and phenological dynamics, often treat climate drivers (temperature, precipitation, radiation) as independent additive factors. This simplification is at odds with ecological theory and observational evidence, limiting our ability to predict recovery trajectories under novel climate regimes emerging from climate change. This gap is critical for California ecosystems, where increasing fire frequency interacts with climate vulnerability, threatening carbon sequestration and community composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interactive, non-additive effects of temperature, precipitation, and solar radiation are primary determinants of post-fire phenological recovery rates and that these interactions are modulated by pre-fire vegetation type and soil microbial community composition.
proposals.method: We will integrate remote sensing phenology data (MODIS/VIIRS) with high-resolution climate grids (PRISM) and a database of post-fire recovery studies from California. Using a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework, we will develop a model where climate drivers interact via multiplicative terms and threshold functions. The model will be parameterized separately for key vegetation functional types (e.g., low-elevation conifers, chaparral) and will incorporate soil microbial functional gene data (from 16S/ITS and metagenomics) related to carbon degradation as a moderating variable influencing the climate-phenology relationship.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, empirically-grounded model of post-fire phenology that explicitly quantifies climate driver interactions, moving beyond simple additive approaches. It will directly inform predictions of ecosystem carbon balance and vulnerability under future fire regimes. The integration of microbial community data will provide a mechanistic link between soil processes and canopy recovery.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has documented climate impacts on phenology and post-fire recovery separately, the explicit modeling of interactive climate effects on post-fire phenology is novel. Combining this with microbial functional data to explain residual variance offers a new, cross-scale explanatory framework.

proposals.title: High-Resolution CASA Modeling Reveals Fine-Scale Post-Fire Carbon Dynamics and Vulnerability in California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Current assessments of wildfire impacts on ecosystem carbon balance, such as those using the Carnegie-Ames-Stanford Approach (CASA) model, often rely on coarse-resolution (~1 km) climate and satellite data. This aggregation obscures critical heterogeneity in post-fire recovery and vulnerability, particularly in topographically complex regions like California's Sierra Nevada, where Field's recent work has documented climate disequilibrium. Finer-scale data is essential to accurately quantify carbon fluxes and identify ecosystem tipping points under changing fire regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that running the CASA model with sub-kilometer, daily-to-weekly resolution climate and satellite inputs will significantly reduce aggregation errors, revealing that post-fire photosynthetic recovery is slower and more spatially variable, and that carbon vulnerability is higher, in fine-scale climate refugia and transition zones than previously estimated.
proposals.method: We will implement the CASA ecosystem model at a 250m spatial and 8-day temporal resolution for California's fire-prone regions from 2001-2025. Inputs will include: 1) MODIS/VIIRS-derived NDVI/EVI and land surface temperature at native 250m/375m resolution; 2) downscaled daily PRISM climate data (precipitation, temperature); 3) high-resolution soil maps. The model will be driven with historical fire perimeters (e.g., MTBS) to partition grids into burned/unburned fractions. We will validate modeled NPP and soil carbon against flux tower data and field campaigns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, long-term assessment of post-fire carbon balance for California, directly quantifying aggregation biases in state-wide carbon budgets. It will provide a mechanistic, spatially explicit framework to identify ecosystems most vulnerable to carbon loss from compound fire-climate stresses, informing management priorities for carbon resilience.
proposals.novelty: While CASA is a established model, its application at this fine spatiotemporal scale to explicitly resolve post-fire carbon fluxes across heterogeneous landscapes integrates Field's recent themes of fire, climate disequilibrium, and carbon cycling in a novel, data-rich framework that moves beyond regional averages.

proposals.title: Global Terrestrial NPP Responses to Climate Anomalies: Quantifying the Roles of Fire, Soil Carbon, and Microbial-Mediated Nutrient Cycling
proposals.motivation: Interannual variability in net primary production (NPP) is a critical but poorly constrained component of the global carbon cycle, influencing carbon sequestration and ecosystem resilience. Major climate modes like El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) drive global-scale climate anomalies, yet their integrated impacts on NPP—mediated by fire regimes, soil carbon dynamics, and nutrient deposition—are not fully understood, limiting our ability to predict carbon-climate feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sensitivity of global terrestrial NPP to ENSO-driven climate anomalies is non-uniformly amplified in fire-prone ecosystems, where post-fire shifts in soil microbial communities and nutrient availability constrain photosynthetic recovery rates.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-year (2001-2025) global simulation using the Community Land Model (CLM5.0) coupled to a prognostic fire module. The core innovation is the integration of a microbial-explicit soil decomposition model (MIMICS) and a dynamic vegetation module (FATES) to represent post-fire succession. Simulations will be forced with MERRA-2 climate data, isolating ENSO years. We will validate modeled NPP and fire patterns against MODIS-derived products (MOD17A3H, MCD64A1) and leverage global soil metagenomic databases (e.g., EMP) to parameterize post-fire microbial functional gene shifts related to carbon and nitrogen degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first globally gridded, process-based assessment of how fire-climate interactions modulate interannual NPP variability. It will quantify the contribution of microbial-mediated nutrient cycling to post-fire carbon recovery, directly informing dynamic global vegetation models. The findings will advance our mechanistic understanding of ecosystem vulnerability to compound climate disturbances.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies correlate NPP with climate indices, this proposal uniquely integrates fire disturbance, microbial functional genomics, and soil carbon dynamics within a unified modeling framework to mechanistically explain global NPP variability, moving beyond statistical descriptions.

proposals.title: A Spatially Explicit SIR-Type Model for Simulating Fire-Driven Vegetation Transitions and Carbon Vulnerability in California
proposals.motivation: Climate change is increasing fire frequency and intensity in California, threatening to push ecosystems past tipping points and fundamentally alter carbon storage. Current dynamic global vegetation models (DGVMs) often lack the mechanistic, spatially explicit representation of disturbance propagation and post-fire recovery needed to project these non-linear shifts. Integrating epidemiological modeling concepts offers a novel framework to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatially explicit compartment model, adapted from epidemiology, can more accurately simulate the rates and patterns of fire-driven vegetation transitions and associated carbon loss in California than standard DGVM formulations, by explicitly capturing disturbance contagion and state-dependent recovery.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) framework to a gridded landscape, where each cell exists in states: Susceptible (immature/mature vegetation), Infected (actively burning), and Recovered (recently disturbed/regenerating). Transition probabilities between states will be governed by climate-driven fuel moisture, vegetation-specific flammability, and microbial-mediated decomposition rates for post-fire carbon loss. The model will be implemented in a probabilistic cellular automaton, with fire spread as a neighbor interaction function. We will parameterize and validate the model using pre-2026 California fire history, climate, and vegetation plot data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, parsimonious modeling tool to project landscape-scale vulnerability to fire-induced vegetation type conversion. It will directly quantify how altered fire regimes disrupt the carbon cycle, linking disturbance dynamics to soil and ecosystem carbon pools, a core focus of Field's research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical translation of epidemiological contagion models to ecological disturbance propagation, moving beyond simple area-burned statistics to simulate internal dynamics of disturbance and recovery within a unified, probabilistic framework.

proposals.title: Dynamic Vegetation-Fire Feedbacks in a Changing Climate: Integrating Plant Functional Type Shifts, Land-Use, and Disturbance Regimes to Project California's Carbon Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Field's work, shows California ecosystems are vulnerable to climate-driven increases in fire frequency and are often out of equilibrium with climate. Current dynamic global vegetation models (DGVMs) often treat vegetation shifts, land-use change, and fire disturbance as semi-independent processes, limiting projections of ecosystem carbon storage and community composition under combined stressors. A unified framework is needed to capture the feedbacks where fire alters plant functional type (PFT) dominance, which in turn modifies fuel loads and future fire risk, especially under anthropogenic land-use pressures.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating dynamic PFT competition, explicit fire disturbance, and land-use change scenarios within a process-based model will reveal non-linear feedbacks that significantly alter projections of regional carbon stocks and vegetation community resilience compared to models treating these drivers in isolation.
proposals.method: We will extend the Community Land Model (CLM) with its DGVM and fire modules (e.g., SPITFIRE), incorporating pre-2026 algorithms for PFT competition based on traits related to post-fire recovery (e.g., resprouting, seed banking). Land-use change will be implemented via the Land-Use Harmonization (LUH2) dataset scenarios. The core technical integration involves modifying the model architecture to allow fire events to directly modify PFT fractional coverage and trait parameters (e.g., flammability, decomposition rates) in a spatially explicit manner. Model validation will leverage pre-2026 remote sensing data on post-fire photosynthetic recovery and historical vegetation shifts in California.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated modeling framework that quantifies the synergistic effects of climate, fire, and land-use on vegetation transitions and carbon cycling. It will provide spatially explicit projections of future ecosystem states and carbon sequestration potential for California, directly informing land management and climate mitigation strategies. The model will advance mechanistic understanding of ecosystem vulnerability and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While DGVMs and fire models exist, the explicit, two-way coupling of dynamic PFT successional rules (informed by microbial degradation and trait data) with fire disturbance algorithms and land-use transitions within a single, regionally calibrated framework for California represents a significant integration beyond standard practice pre-2026.

proposals.title: Integrating Process and Pattern: A Hybrid Machine Learning-Geostatistical Framework for High-Resolution Global Soil Carbon and Nitrogen Mapping
proposals.motivation: Accurate, high-resolution maps of soil carbon and nitrogen are critical for quantifying terrestrial carbon stocks, understanding vulnerability to climate change and disturbance, and informing land management. Existing global products, like SoilGrids, primarily rely on machine learning with environmental covariates, but often fail to capture fine-scale, spatially structured variation not explained by these drivers, which is crucial for applications in fire-prone ecosystems and restoration planning. This limitation hinders our ability to predict post-disturbance biogeochemical trajectories and assess landscape-scale heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid modeling framework, which explicitly separates covariate-driven predictions from spatially autocorrelated residuals, will produce more accurate and spatially faithful global maps of soil carbon and nitrogen stocks than purely covariate-based machine learning models.
proposals.method: We will train an ensemble of gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) on a global soil observation database (e.g., WoSIS) using pre-2026 standard covariates: climate (CHELSA), satellite-derived vegetation (MODIS EVI/NDVI), topography (SRTM), land use (ESA CCI), and soil texture estimates. The spatial residuals from this model will be analyzed for global and regional spatial autocorrelation using variograms. Where significant spatial structure exists (e.g., nugget-to-sill ratio < 0.8), we will perform regression kriging on the residuals at a 250m resolution. The final map is the sum of the machine learning prediction and the kriged residual surface.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available global soil carbon and nitrogen product with quantified uncertainty that better represents fine-scale spatial heterogeneity. It will provide a methodological advance for the digital soil mapping community by formally integrating process-informed covariates with empirical spatial patterns. The resulting maps will directly benefit research on fire-carbon feedbacks, ecosystem recovery, and regional carbon budgeting.
proposals.novelty: While regression kriging is established in pedometrics, its application at the global scale for soil biogeochemistry has been limited, and the explicit diagnostic testing for residual spatial structure before kriging represents a principled departure from purely covariate-driven global models. This hybrid approach directly addresses the 'spatial indifference' of standard machine learning models in soil mapping.

proposals.title: Quantifying Ecosystem Carbon Resilience: An Input-Output Network Analysis of Post-Fire Recovery in California
proposals.motivation: Understanding the stability of terrestrial carbon stocks is critical for climate mitigation, yet current models often treat carbon pools as static or use descriptive network maps. The increasing frequency and severity of wildfires in California, a major perturbation to carbon flows, demands a framework that can quantitatively predict cascading impacts on ecosystem carbon balance and recovery trajectories, directly informing land management and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying economic Input-Output (I-O) network analysis to ecosystem carbon fluxes will reveal that post-fire carbon flow stability is governed by a small number of key carbon pools with high multiplier effects, and that these hubs are disproportionately vulnerable to climate-driven changes in vegetation and microbial degradation pathways.
proposals.method: We will construct carbon I-O tables for major California ecosystems (e.g., Sierra Nevada conifer forests) by synthesizing pre-2026 data on pool sizes (soil, live biomass, litter) and directed fluxes (NPP, respiration, fire emissions, leaching). Using the Leontief model, we will compute the matrix of total requirement coefficients (Leontief inverse) to quantify carbon output multipliers for each pool. Network stability will be tested by simulating node (pool) and edge (flux) perturbations analogous to economic 'shocks,' such as fire-induced vegetation loss or microbial degradation shifts, modeled using pre-2026 climate and genomic data on microbial functional traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, network-based metric of carbon flow stability in fire-prone ecosystems, identifying leverage points for management. It will directly advance the field by offering a novel framework to project the pace of photosynthetic and soil carbon recovery after disturbance, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic, system-level vulnerability assessment.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious adaptation lies in formally translating the Leontief inverse—a core tool for analyzing economic ripple effects—into an ecological context to calculate carbon multipliers and simulate cascading failures, a significant advance over existing ecological network analyses that focus on topology or energy flow without this rigorous stability calculus.

proposals.title: Mapping the Fragility of the Terrestrial Carbon Sink: A Spatially-Explicit Network Analysis of Soil Carbon Fluxes in Fire-Prone Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Terrestrial carbon sinks are critical for climate mitigation but are increasingly destabilized by disturbances like wildfire, which are intensifying globally. Current Earth system models treat carbon pools and fluxes in coarse, aggregated grid cells, obscuring how local heterogeneity in soil carbon and microbial processes governs system-wide resilience. This gap limits our ability to predict how disturbances propagate and where management interventions could most effectively protect vulnerable carbon stocks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution spatial fields of soil carbon and nitrogen with ecological network analysis will reveal specific geographic hotspots where the carbon flow network is most fragile to perturbations like fire-induced deposition changes or microbial community shifts, and that these hotspots are not predictable from mean pool sizes alone.
proposals.method: We will apply Bayesian geostatistical kriging to globally available soil (e.g., SoilGrids) and climate data to generate probabilistic, continuous maps of carbon and nitrogen pools at 1-km resolution for target regions like California's Sierra Nevada. These maps define network nodes. Directed edges (fluxes) will be modeled using process-based functions for microbial decomposition and vegetation inputs, parameterized with metagenomic and biogeochemical data from recent post-fire studies. Network stability metrics (e.g., loop weight, node impact) will be calculated using Ecological Network Analysis (ENA) and mapped spatially to identify fragility hotspots.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially continuous fragility map of terrestrial carbon flows, directly linking local soil properties to global-scale network resilience. It will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for prioritizing landscape management and forecasting how localized disturbances or restoration actions propagate through the carbon cycle.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of geostatistical kriging with Ecological Network Analysis moves beyond abstract, compartmentalized models to represent carbon flow stability as an explicit, mappable geographic field, a significant advance over pre-2026 modeling paradigms.

proposals.title: Network Stability of Post-Fire Carbon Cycling: Integrating Microbial Functional Genes and Vegetation Recovery in California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Increasing fire frequency in California, as documented in Field's recent work, threatens the stability of critical ecosystem carbon (C) pools. Current models often treat C pools as static boxes, failing to capture the complex, directed fluxes between vegetation, soil, and microbial communities that determine system resilience. A network-based analysis is needed to predict how fire regimes shift C flow pathways and potentially push ecosystems toward irreversible C loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-fire ecosystem C flow networks become less stable and more directed toward respiratory losses, and that this instability is mechanistically explained by the decoupling of plant-derived C inputs from microbial functional gene networks responsible for decomposition versus stabilization.
proposals.method: We will construct directed C flow networks for key California ecosystems (e.g., Sierra Nevada conifers, chaparral) using pre-2026 flux tower data, soil respiration measurements, and allometric equations. Nodes will represent C pools (foliage, wood, litter, microbial biomass, mineral-associated organic matter). Edge weights will be flux rates. We will apply ecological network analysis (ENA) metrics, specifically using the `enaR` package in R to compute ascendency, development capacity, and Finn's Cycling Index. This topological analysis will be integrated with field data on microbial functional genes (e.g., for lignin degradation, pyrogenic C transformation) and vegetation photosynthetic recovery rates to attribute network changes to specific biological mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for quantifying ecosystem C stability, moving beyond pool sizes to flux dynamics. It will directly link fire-driven changes in plant communities and microbial functional potential to systemic vulnerabilities in the C cycle, offering new metrics for predicting C sink-to-source transitions under changing climate and fire regimes.
proposals.novelty: While ENA has been used in food webs, its application to ecosystem C fluxes is nascent, and no study has yet coupled it with genomic data on microbial functional potential to explain network stability in fire-prone ecosystems. This integrates Field's expertise in fire, vegetation recovery, and microbial processes into a unified analytical framework.

proposals.title: Robust Carbon Storage in California Forests: A State-and-Transition Simulation Framework for Optimizing Resilience to Altered Fire and Climate Regimes
proposals.motivation: Climate change is increasing wildfire frequency and intensity in California, threatening to convert long-term forest carbon sinks into sources. Current carbon management often targets static stocks, but this approach is vulnerable to abrupt, non-linear losses from compound disturbances. A dynamic resilience framework is urgently needed to identify management strategies that maintain carbon storage across multiple possible future states and disturbance pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Management strategies that optimize for the long-term dynamic resilience of ecosystem carbon, rather than its maximum static stock, will yield higher and more stable carbon storage across a wide range of future climate and disturbance scenarios. This resilience is achieved by promoting successional pathways and community compositions that recover carbon rapidly post-disturbance.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-and-transition simulation (STS) model parameterized for California's Sierra Nevada conifer systems, integrating pre-2026 data on vegetation recovery, soil carbon dynamics, and microbial degradation genes post-fire. The model will incorporate probabilistic fire regimes and climate projections. We will use a Monte Carlo simulation approach coupled with a reinforcement learning algorithm (e.g., Q-learning) to evaluate and optimize management actions (e.g., fuel reduction, assisted migration) across thousands of stochastic futures, identifying policies that maximize a resilience metric defined as the time-integrated carbon storage over a 100-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for resilient forest carbon management that explicitly accounts for disturbance legacies and successional trade-offs. It will deliver specific, robust management policies for California forests and a generalizable method to quantify carbon resilience, moving the field beyond equilibrium-based stock assessments.
proposals.novelty: This reframes the carbon optimization problem from a static maximization to a dynamic control theory problem under deep uncertainty. It uniquely integrates genetic markers of microbial decomposition, probabilistic disturbance modeling, and machine learning-based policy search within an ecological state-transition framework to find robust solutions.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Carbon-Risk Frontier: A Portfolio Theory Approach to Post-Fire Ecosystem Recovery in California
proposals.motivation: In fire-prone California ecosystems, post-fire recovery involves a trade-off between rapid carbon sequestration and the stability of that carbon stock against future disturbances and climate stress. Current models of ecosystem carbon dynamics often treat this trade-off implicitly. Formalizing this as an optimization problem is critical for predicting and managing landscapes for both carbon storage and resilience, especially under changing fire regimes and climate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the post-fire recovery trajectory of ecosystem carbon storage can be modeled as a mean-variance optimization, where the plant community composition that maximizes carbon gain for a given level of risk (variance from climate vulnerability and fire) lies on an 'efficient frontier' analogous to financial portfolios.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Markowitz mean-variance optimization framework. Using pre-2026 data from California post-fire chronosequences (e.g., from Field's prior work), we will parameterize plant functional types as 'assets' with expected 'returns' (net carbon assimilation rates from eddy covariance/remote sensing) and 'risk' (temporal variance in productivity from climate sensitivity and fire vulnerability indices). Resource constraints (water, nutrients) will define the feasible set. The optimization will be solved using quadratic programming (e.g., the 'quadprog' package in R) to identify the theoretical efficient frontier of community compositions under different resource and risk-tolerance scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework to diagnose whether recovering ecosystems are on an optimal path for carbon storage given inherent risks. It will yield testable predictions for ideal species mixes to enhance managed restoration outcomes. The approach formally bridges ecological theory and financial mathematics to address a core scaling problem in biogeochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs are central to ecology, directly applying portfolio optimization's formal risk-return calculus to ecosystem carbon storage is novel. This moves beyond descriptive correlations to a prescriptive, optimization-based theory for community assembly following disturbance.

proposals.title: Light-Driven Network Stability: Integrating Radiative Transfer and Ecological Network Analysis to Predict Post-Fire Carbon Cycle Resilience in California Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem carbon sequestration stability is threatened by increasing climate-driven disturbances like wildfire. Pre-2026 research shows post-fire photosynthetic recovery is highly variable, but we lack a mechanistic framework linking the fundamental energetic driver—light—to the stability of the entire carbon flow network. This gap limits our ability to predict how changes in the atmospheric and canopy light regime, from smoke or altered phenology, affect ecosystem resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stability and modularity of ecosystem carbon flow networks are directly governed by the spatial heterogeneity and temporal variability of Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR), and that post-fire reductions in network stability can be predicted by coupling light-driven assimilation with belowground carbon pool dynamics.
proposals.method: We will fuse radiative transfer model-downscaled PAR (from pre-2026 platforms like MODIS/GOES) with a directed ecological network model of carbon pools (foliage, wood, roots, soil, microbial biomass) and fluxes. Carbon assimilation at vegetation nodes will be dynamically forced by high-resolution, canopy-level PAR. We will apply Ecological Network Analysis (ENA) algorithms, specifically calculating ascendency, robustness, and Finn's cycling index, to simulated carbon flows across a gradient of California ecosystems with pre- and post-fire conditions from Field's recent work. Network stability metrics will be analyzed against PAR heterogeneity indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistic framework that quantifies how the atmospheric light environment controls carbon cycle stability. It will directly link Field's recent findings on post-fire photosynthetic recovery to whole-ecosystem carbon resilience. The resulting model will be a new tool for projecting ecosystem vulnerability to compounded disturbances like drought, fire, and smoke.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of physically downscaled radiative transfer data as the dynamic driver for ecological network analysis of carbon flows—a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond static or climate-forced models to a direct energy-flux-driven stability assessment.

ideas.title: Spatially Explicit Projection of Soil Carbon Vulnerability from NPP-Driven Input Changes
ideas.description: This research integrates geostatistical kriging of soil carbon and nitrogen input data with models of progressive Net Primary Production (NPP) change to generate high-resolution, probabilistic forecasts of altered soil carbon storage. It moves beyond simple NPP-to-storage multipliers by using the spatially interpolated input maps as a critical baseline, allowing the model to account for heterogeneous landscape responsiveness to NPP shifts based on underlying soil-forming factors. The synergy enables the quantification of not just the global carbon outcome, but the identification of specific geographic hotspots where soil carbon stocks are most vulnerable (or resilient) to changes in primary production, thereby directly informing land management and climate mitigation policy.

ideas.title: DynRes: Modeling carbon storage as a dynamic resilience optimization under disturbance regimes
ideas.description: This approach reframes ecosystem carbon management as a problem of optimizing for long-term stability and resilience, rather than simply maximizing a static stock. It uses a state-and-transition simulation framework that integrates probabilistic disturbances, successional pathways, and legacy carbon effects to model non-linear temporal dynamics. The system evaluates management strategies against a wide range of possible future climate and disturbance scenarios to identify robust, adaptive policies. The goal is to find interventions that maintain high carbon storage across multiple ecosystem states and through time, not just at a theoretical equilibrium point.

ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution satellite data with process-based models of interactive limitations to create a new generation of diagnostic tools. The core advance is the explicit, spatially resolved simulation of co-limitation regimes and transient limitation states, moving beyond static, single-factor explanations for NPP patterns. This will allow for the mechanistic attribution of observed heterogeneity in global NPP to specific, interacting environmental drivers at ecologically relevant scales. The resulting framework will significantly improve predictions of how NPP and its controlling factors will respond to climate variability and change.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Dynamic Allocation: Quantifying Carbon Partitioning Uncertainty in Response to Environmental Stress
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic carbon allocation models with Monte Carlo ensemble methods to propagate uncertainty from both input data (e.g., soil nutrient maps, precipitation forecasts) and the allocation response functions themselves. By running thousands of simulations where allocation coefficients are sampled from probability distributions shaped by environmental drivers, the model generates a probabilistic cloud of possible biomass outcomes (roots, wood, leaves) rather than a single prediction. This allows for the identification of high-risk environmental thresholds where allocation strategies become highly uncertain, and for assessing the probability of critical failures like insufficient root investment during drought. The approach provides a new, uncertainty-aware lens on plant functional traits and their resilience under global change.

ideas.title: Network Stability of Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)-Driven Carbon Flows
ideas.description: This research integrates radiative transfer-downscaled PAR data with ecological network analysis to create dynamic, light-driven models of carbon flow. By using high-resolution, canopy-level PAR as the primary energetic driver for carbon assimilation nodes within a directed network of ecosystem pools and fluxes, we can simulate how spatial heterogeneity in light availability propagates through the carbon cycle. This approach allows for the analysis of network stability metrics (e.g., robustness, modularity) in direct response to realistic patterns of cloud cover, canopy structure, and phenology. The result is a novel framework for predicting how changes in the atmospheric light regime affect the stability and resilience of ecosystem carbon sequestration functions.

ideas.title: Dual: Ecosystem Carbon Storage as a Portfolio Optimization Problem
ideas.description: This idea frames ecosystem production as maximizing carbon gain under resource constraints by drawing a direct analogy from financial portfolio theory. In that field, the mean-variance optimization framework solves the problem of allocating capital among assets to maximize return for a chosen risk level. We adapt this by treating an ecosystem's plant community as a portfolio of 'assets' (species or functional types), where the 'return' is the rate of carbon sequestration and 'risk' is the temporal variance or vulnerability of that carbon stock. The analogous solution provides a formal optimization structure to identify the species composition and trait distribution that lies on the 'efficient frontier' of carbon storage—maximizing sequestration for a defined level of resilience or stability, given finite resources like water, light, and nutrients. This transforms the scaling dual problem into a quantifiable trade-off analysis between carbon gain and ecosystem risk.

ideas.title: Spatially-Explicit Ecological Network Analysis of Global Carbon Flows
ideas.description: This research direction integrates geostatistical kriging with ecological network analysis to construct spatially explicit, high-resolution models of terrestrial carbon flows. It uses kriging to generate continuous, probabilistic maps of soil carbon and nitrogen pools, which serve as the spatially distributed nodes in a directed network where fluxes are modeled as edges. This allows for the analysis of carbon flow stability and resilience not just as an abstract system property, but as a spatially continuous field, identifying geographic hotspots of network fragility and robust carbon pathways. Consequently, it provides a novel framework for predicting how local soil disturbances or management changes might propagate through the carbon network at regional to global scales.

ideas.title: Adapting Input-Output Network Analysis from Economics to Carbon Flow Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Economic Systems Analysis, specifically Input-Output (I-O) network modeling, to the original context of analyzing carbon flows. In economics, I-O analysis quantifies the interdependence of industrial sectors through directed flows of goods and money, using the Leontief model to calculate total output requirements and assess network stability against shocks. The adapted research idea applies this formal network methodology to ecological carbon pools and fluxes. By constructing a carbon I-O table and computing the analogous 'Leontief inverse' for the carbon network, we can move beyond descriptive mapping to quantitatively analyze system stability, identify pools with high multiplier effects (key carbon hubs), and simulate the cascading impacts of perturbations like deforestation or sink saturation on the overall carbon balance, thereby providing a robust analytical framework for ecosystem resilience and climate mitigation planning.

ideas.title: Modeling Synergistic Climate Stress on Phenology via Pharmacodynamic Interaction Frameworks
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of independent, additive effects of climate drivers on plant phenology by drawing an analogy from pharmacology, where the combined effects of multiple drugs are explicitly modeled for interaction. The analogous solution uses response surface methodologies and interaction indices (e.g., Loewe additivity) to characterize synergistic, additive, or antagonistic effects. Adapted to climate science, the research would develop a modeling framework where temperature, solar radiation, and precipitation are treated as interacting agents. The goal is to fit empirical phenological response surfaces to observational data, calculating interaction indices to map and predict where and how these drivers combine in non-linear, non-additive ways to advance or delay key phenological events, fundamentally improving the mechanistic understanding of plant responses to concurrent climate stresses.

ideas.title: A Liebig-Limitation Network Model for Interactive NPP Constraints
ideas.description: Develop a model that simulates the interactive effects of light, nutrients, temperature, and water on Net Primary Production (NPP) by drawing an analogy from systems pharmacology. The analogous problem—predicting the combined effect of multiple drugs—is solved using response surface models (e.g., Bliss Independence) that map synergistic, additive, or antagonistic interactions. The adapted idea applies this framework to ecosystem science by treating each environmental factor as an 'agent' influencing the 'system response' of NPP. The model would generate multi-dimensional response surfaces (isoboles of equal NPP) to visualize and quantify how co-limitation and interactive effects emerge across environmental gradients, providing a more mechanistic and predictive understanding of NPP constraints under global change.

ideas.title: Hybrid covariate-driven and residual kriging for global soil biogeochemical maps
ideas.description: This system first uses a machine learning model trained on globally available environmental covariates (climate, satellite-derived vegetation indices, land use, topography, and soil texture estimates) to predict soil carbon and nitrogen. The spatial residuals from this model are then analyzed for spatial structure and interpolated via kriging if significant autocorrelation exists. The final map is the sum of the covariate-based prediction and the interpolated residuals, explicitly separating large-scale, process-driven variation from fine-scale, spatially structured noise. This approach ensures predictions are anchored in known ecological drivers while still leveraging spatial patterns where they provide genuine information gain.

ideas.title: Dual-Ensemble Data Assimilation for Joint State-Parameter Learning in Hybrid Ecological Models
ideas.description: This research develops a novel data assimilation framework specifically designed for the structural uncertainties in ecological models like those for Net Primary Production (NPP). Instead of just correcting model states, the system uses a dual-ensemble approach to simultaneously constrain poorly known ecological parameters (e.g., light-use efficiency, carbon allocation coefficients) alongside traditional state variables. This joint estimation is embedded within a hybrid model that can progressively replace poorly constrained empirical sub-models with more mechanistic components informed by the assimilation process. The result is a self-improving modeling system that not only fits diverse satellite and in-situ data better but also evolves toward a more robust and generalizable representation of ecosystem function.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartment Models for Dynamic Vegetation and Disturbance
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the model by adapting solution concepts from epidemiology. The analogous field is Epidemiology, where compartmental models (e.g., SIR - Susceptible, Infected, Recovered) dynamically simulate disease spread through populations based on transition rates. The analogous solution involves tracking individuals moving between health states due to transmission and recovery, influenced by external interventions. This is adapted to the research context by treating landscape grid cells as compartments in states like susceptible immature, mature, disturbed, and regenerating vegetation. Transitions between these states are governed by probabilistic rules for growth, disturbance agents (fire, land-use change), and succession, allowing disturbances to propagate based on neighbor interactions and environmental drivers. This framework provides a robust method to dynamically integrate vegetation lifecycles, disturbance effects, and land-use change, simulating complex shifts in plant functional types across a landscape.

ideas.title: Data-Assisted Transient Spin-Up for Terrestrial Carbon Models
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the relaxation of the steady-state assumption with data assimilation techniques to develop a novel 'transient spin-up' procedure for models like CASA. Instead of initializing from an artificial equilibrium, the model is initialized from a historically plausible non-steady state, which is generated by running the model backwards in time while assimilating past satellite and flux tower data via an ensemble Kalman filter. This process simultaneously optimizes initial pool sizes and model parameters against historical observations, creating a foundation state that is consistent with both the model physics and the observed trajectory of the carbon cycle. The resulting initialized model provides a more accurate baseline for forecasting carbon fluxes and pool responses to future climate variability and extremes.

ideas.title: Apply Ensemble Kalman Filtering from Reservoir Engineering to CASA Model Parameter Calibration
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Petroleum Reservoir Engineering—specifically, the use of Ensemble Kalman Filtering (EnKF) for reservoir history matching—to the problem of calibrating terrestrial carbon cycle models. In reservoir engineering, EnKF assimilates sparse production data into high-dimensional simulation models to update uncertain parameters (like permeability) and quantify prediction uncertainty. The adapted research idea applies this EnKF framework to the CASA biogeochemical model. It involves using an ensemble of CASA model realizations with varying parameters to assimilate time-series data from satellites (e.g., soil moisture) and flux towers (e.g., net ecosystem exchange). The EnKF will dynamically calibrate critical but uncertain CASA parameters, such as temperature and moisture stress coefficients, providing a continuous, data-driven optimization that improves carbon flux forecasts and explicitly quantifies their uncertainty, mirroring the parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification goals of the reservoir engineering analogue.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for CASA Model Inputs Using Multi-Scale Satellite Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the challenge of incorporating higher-resolution satellite and climate data into the CASA model. In CFD, AMR solves the problem of computational cost versus accuracy by dynamically refining the simulation grid only in critical regions. The adapted solution is to create a dynamic data ingestion framework for CASA that uses a multi-scale approach: it employs a baseline moderate-resolution climate and satellite data grid, then algorithmically identifies regions of high spatial or temporal heterogeneity (using variance metrics or edge detection on prior runs) and selectively fuses in the finest available resolution data streams for those specific areas. This targeted approach reduces aggregation errors in the most sensitive parts of the landscape, optimizing the trade-off between model fidelity and the computational cost of processing petabyte-scale, high-resolution global datasets.

ideas.title: Constrained emergent carbon allocation from resource economics and measurable plant traits
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply making allocation coefficients variable by embedding them within a testable, trait-based framework of plant resource economics. Allocation to roots, wood, and leaves emerges from solving a numerical optimization problem that balances the costs of constructing and maintaining tissues against their benefits for acquiring limiting resources like water, nutrients, and light. The model is constrained by measurable traits (e.g., specific root length, leaf mass per area, hydraulic conductivity) and fundamental trade-offs, ensuring that predicted allocation shifts are both physiologically plausible and mechanistically explicit. This allows the model to be rigorously validated against independent data on trait plasticity and environmental response, producing robust predictions for novel environments.

ideas.title: Inferring Dynamic Biomass Allocation from Satellite Phenology using Process-Guided Machine Learning
ideas.description: This research fuses satellite-based phenology classification with dynamic allocation theory by developing a hybrid process-guided machine learning model. The model uses global NDVI time series as input, but its architecture and training are constrained by mechanistic rules of how nutrient and water stress should shift allocation between roots, wood, and leaves. The output is not just a biome label, but a spatially and temporally explicit estimation of variable allocation coefficients. This enables the first large-scale, observation-based challenge to fixed allocation schemes in ecosystem models directly from remote sensing data.

ideas.title: Process-Explicit, Trait-Based Carbon Flow Networks Integrating Metabolic and Stoichiometric Constraints
ideas.description: This redesigned approach constructs a global carbon flow network using a process-explicit, agent-based framework grounded in ecological first principles. It parameterizes nodes (e.g., plant functional types, microbial guilds, herbivores) using measurable traits and metabolic scaling laws, while edges represent carbon transfers governed by enzyme kinetics, stoichiometric balances, and thermodynamic constraints. The model dynamically simulates nonlinear flows, feedbacks, and threshold behaviors, enabling the tracing of Net Primary Production (NPP) through ecosystems under varying environmental conditions. This creates a more mechanistic and predictive tool for understanding carbon fate than a static, linear input-output analogy.

ideas.title: Adopt Ensemble Kalman Filtering from Numerical Weather Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), a cornerstone method in Numerical Weather Prediction for handling uncertainty, to the context of machine learning. In NWP, EnKF maintains an ensemble of possible atmospheric states to represent forecast uncertainty, propagates it, and updates it with new data. Adapted here, an ensemble of model instances is created, each receiving a version of the input data perturbed within its known uncertainty bounds (e.g., measurement error). This ensemble is propagated through the model to produce a distribution of outputs. Crucially, the method incorporates a data assimilation step: when new, reliable validation data becomes available, it is used to update the ensemble members (akin to an analysis step in EnKF), dynamically reducing output uncertainty. This provides a continuous, probabilistic prediction that rigorously accounts for input uncertainty and can be refined over time.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Carbon Flow Networks via Monte Carlo Input-Output Analysis
ideas.description: This research synthesizes economic input-output network analysis with Monte Carlo methods from particle physics to create a novel probabilistic model of global carbon cycling. The input-output structure traces net primary production (NPP) and carbon flows through ecosystem compartments (nodes) and transfer pathways (edges), defining the system's network topology. Monte Carlo ensemble simulations are then used to propagate quantified uncertainties in input data, node parameters, and edge coefficients through this interconnected network. The result is a dynamic model that outputs not just a single carbon budget, but probability distributions for all stocks and fluxes, revealing systemic vulnerabilities and confidence intervals for critical carbon cycle projections.

ideas.title: Adapting Oceanographic Data Fusion for Global Soil Carbon Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from physical oceanography to improve global soil carbon and nitrogen maps. In oceanography, sparse in-situ measurements are fused with continuous satellite data to create comprehensive 3D models. The adapted research idea applies this principle by using geostatistical kriging (co-kriging or regression kriging) where globally available, high-resolution remote sensing layers (like NDVI, land surface temperature, or topographic indices) serve as secondary variables to guide the spatial interpolation of sparse soil core data for C and N. This leverages the dense spatial coverage of satellites to better predict soil property variations between measurement points, significantly enhancing the accuracy and resolution of the resulting global input maps beyond what is possible with point data alone.

ideas.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Fusing Direct Flux Measurements and Satellite Indices to Derive a Physically-Constrained, Biome-Aware NPP Product
ideas.description: This research develops a neural network architecture that ingests both long-term satellite NDVI time series and concurrent, but spatially sparse, direct measurements of photosynthetic fluxes (e.g., from eddy covariance towers). The model is trained to recognize biome-specific phenological patterns from the NDVI data while simultaneously learning the complex, non-linear, and biome-dependent relationships between these optical indices and the true absorbed radiation used for NPP. The synergy produces a globally applicable model that can extrapolate the physical constraint of direct flux measurements across space and time, generating an NPP product that is more accurate than index-based proxies and more spatially complete than flux measurements alone.

ideas.description: This idea overcomes the prohibitive computational cost of native high-resolution CASA runs by developing a hybrid modeling system. It uses targeted, high-resolution CASA simulations to train a fast statistical emulator that can predict high-resolution carbon fluxes from coarser, globally available driver data. This emulator enables the generation of large ensembles at high spatial and temporal resolution, allowing for robust quantification of uncertainties stemming from both model parameters and input data. The final output is a high-resolution, probabilistic carbon flux product that is both computationally feasible and scientifically robust.

ideas.title: Validate NEP models using a distributed sensor fusion network inspired by seismology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismology, where dense, heterogeneous sensor networks are used to validate and refine earthquake models. The analogous problem of validating seasonal NEP predictions with sparse atmospheric CO2 data is addressed by proposing a 'sensor fusion' network for carbon. A denser global array of CO2 measurement platforms—including expanded ground stations, portable sensors, and enhanced commercial vessel and aircraft instrumentation—would be deployed. Data from this heterogeneous network would be assimilated using atmospheric inverse models to generate detailed, time-resolved global surface flux maps. These high-resolution, observationally constrained flux maps would then serve as a critical benchmark to rigorously test, validate, and improve the spatial and temporal accuracy of terrestrial biosphere models predicting seasonal NEP.

ideas.title: Adapting Super-Resolution Imaging to Refine NPP Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to refine spatial heterogeneity in global Net Primary Productivity (NPP) by drawing an analogy from super-resolution techniques in medical imaging and microscopy. In that field, the diffraction limit is overcome by fusing multiple low-resolution images to reconstruct a high-resolution one. The adapted idea applies this principle to satellite-based NPP models by creating a computational data fusion framework. This framework would integrate multiple, divergent low-resolution NPP estimates—from different sensors (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS, OLCI), retrieval algorithms (e.g., based on different light-use efficiency parameters or ocean color models), or temporal aggregation methods—to synthetically resolve much finer spatial heterogeneity across land and ocean ecosystems than is possible from any single source, thereby dramatically improving the resolution of global NPP maps.

ideas.title: Adaptive Carbon Budgeting in Plants via Economic Portfolio Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges fixed carbon allocation coefficients by drawing an analogy from financial portfolio management. In that field, capital allocation among assets is dynamically optimized in response to changing market conditions and risk profiles. Adapting this solution, we propose modeling a plant's carbon allocation to roots, wood, and leaves as a dynamic portfolio optimization problem. The plant 'invests' carbon to maximize a fitness objective (e.g., net productivity or reproductive potential) under constraints and stochastic risks posed by varying nutrient and water availability. This results in a mechanistic, responsive allocation model where coefficients are emergent properties of an ongoing optimization process rather than fixed parameters.

ideas.title: Dynamic Substrate Connectivity: A Mechanistic Framework for Soil C-N Coupling Under Hydrological Transients
ideas.description: This research integrates the physical mechanism of substrate diffusion limitation with advanced microbial functional dynamics to move beyond scalar multipliers. It will develop and test a model where soil moisture controls the physical connectivity of organic matter patches to microbial cells, which in turn regulates community-level metabolic strategies and enzyme production for C and N acquisition. This mechanistic link allows the model to predict non-linear pulses of respiration and nitrogen mineralization upon rewetting, as diffusion pathways are suddenly reconnected. The outcome is a unified soil submodule that can simulate how hydrological history and spatial heterogeneity co-determine biogeochemical fluxes.

ideas.title: Downscale CASA using flux tower data with medical imaging reconstruction techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from medical imaging tomographic reconstruction to the problem of downscaling the CASA model to flux tower footprints. In tomography, algorithms like MLEM use a physical forward model and sparse sensor data to reconstruct detailed images. Analogously, we propose to use the eddy covariance measurements as sparse, integrated constraints. The CASA model will serve as the biophysical forward model. An iterative reconstruction framework will be implemented to adjust fine-scale environmental and vegetation drivers within the tower's footprint, minimizing the difference between the model's spatially integrated flux prediction and the tower's measured flux. This yields a rigorously validated, high-resolution reconstruction of carbon fluxes that is consistent with both process theory (CASA) and point observations (eddy covariance), solving the scaling dual problem.

The key innovation is that the model's moisture response function is explicitly reformulated to represent the substrate-diffusion limitation hypothesis, moving beyond a simple empirical scalar. The assimilation process inverts the observational data to optimally constrain the diffusion parameters and their spatial heterogeneity, providing a rigorous, observationally-informed test of the mechanism and producing high-resolution, data-constrained maps of diffusion-limited respiration sensitivity.

ideas.description: This approach integrates satellite and flux tower data into the CASA model using a hybrid ensemble-variational data assimilation framework designed for nonlinear, constrained systems. An ensemble Kalman filter variant handles the propagation of parameter uncertainty, while an embedded iterative variational step imposes hard ecological constraints on parameters, such as realistic bounds and inter-parameter relationships derived from trait databases. This ensures the assimilated parameters remain physically plausible, preventing filter divergence and biophysical nonsense. The system is tested within an Observing System Simulation Experiment (OSSE) framework to rigorously evaluate its performance against known truth scenarios before application to real data.

By training on fused data from multiple satellite platforms (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2, MODIS) and using adversarial learning to penalize features predictive of data source or coarse geography, the model learns transferable representations of phenology. This approach ensures robust classification performance across unseen biomes and when applied to data from new or different satellite sensors, overcoming a key failure mode of single-source models.

ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from seismology, where deep learning models decompose complex seismic signals into interpretable components, to the problem of classifying biomes from NDVI time series. In seismology, convolutional neural networks and autoencoders are used to separate distinct seismic phases and noise from continuous waveform data. Adapted to remote sensing, we propose training a similar neural network architecture to directly decompose a raw satellite NDVI pixel-level time series into its constituent, biome-specific phenological signals (e.g., greening onset, peak photosynthetic activity, senescence). This automated decomposition would simultaneously filter noise and extract the key temporal patterns needed for robust biome classification, moving beyond heuristic curve-fitting or simple statistical metrics.

ideas.title: Applying Percolation Theory to Model Substrate Diffusion Limitation in Soil
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that soil moisture controls decomposition through a simple, continuous scalar relationship with microbial activity. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where electrical conductivity in composites is governed by percolation thresholds and network connectivity, we adapt this framework to soil biogeochemistry. The analogous solution involves modeling the system's connectivity rather than its average state. The adapted research idea is to apply percolation theory to model substrate diffusion limitation in soil. It posits that microbial respiration is limited not by moisture content per se, but by whether the aqueous phase forms a percolating network that enables substrate diffusion to microbial cells. The experiment would test for a critical, non-linear threshold in respiration rates corresponding to the formation of this connected pathway network, fundamentally inverting the classical view of moisture control.

ideas.title: Amortized, Single-Pass Uncertainty Propagation with Consistency Ensembles
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the computational bottleneck of large Monte Carlo ensembles by learning to predict output distributions directly. A secondary, lightweight network is attached to the primary model's penultimate layer and trained to output the parameters of a Gaussian distribution over the final logits or predictions. Training uses a consistency loss that forces this predicted distribution to match the empirical distribution of a tiny, stochastically sampled reference ensemble evaluated only periodically. This provides efficient, amortized uncertainty propagation during inference with a single forward pass, making it scalable to large models and datasets while retaining rigorous probabilistic grounding.

ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography to downscale satellite-derived Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR). In seismology, sparse surface data is inverted using wave propagation physics to image subsurface structures. Analogously, we treat the coarse satellite PAR observation as a low-resolution integral measurement. By using a detailed atmospheric radiative transfer model as the physical forward operator within an inversion framework, we can solve for the high-resolution PAR at the canopy level, effectively 'tomographically' reconstructing the radiation field by accounting for atmospheric scattering, absorption, and canopy architecture effects that modulate the signal between the top of the atmosphere and the target surface.
